rounds.blurb: Orville Wright pilots the first powered flight at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina, while Wilbur runs alongside.
rounds.credit: John T. Daniels / Library of Congress — public domain
rounds.story: The flight lasted twelve seconds and covered 120 feet — shorter than a 747's wingspan. John Daniels, a lifeguard who had never worked a camera before, squeezed the shutter bulb exactly once that morning and captured one of history's most reproduced images.

rounds.blurb: "Earthrise," taken from lunar orbit during Apollo 8 — the first crewed mission to leave Earth orbit.
rounds.story: Coming around the Moon's far side on the fourth orbit, Bill Anders grabbed the only color film aboard and shot Earth rising over the lunar horizon. It has been called the most influential environmental photograph ever taken.

rounds.blurb: UH-1D helicopters lift infantry during Operation Wahiawa, northeast of Cu Chi, South Vietnam.
rounds.credit: James K. F. Dung, SFC, U.S. Army — public domain
rounds.story: The Huey's rotor thump became the signature sound of the war; more than 7,000 served in Vietnam and roughly half never came home. This lift was part of a 25th Infantry Division sweep near Cu Chi, above an immense Viet Cong tunnel network.

rounds.blurb: Babe Ruth in his first season as a Yankee — the year the home run changed baseball.
rounds.story: The Yankees had just bought Ruth from Boston for $125,000; he answered with 54 home runs — more than any other entire team in the American League managed that season. The dead-ball era was over, and Boston's ensuing 86-year drought became the Curse of the Bambino.

rounds.blurb: Space Shuttle Atlantis docked to Russia's Mir station, photographed from a departing Soyuz.
rounds.story: The first shuttle-Mir docking briefly created the largest spacecraft ever assembled — and this photo exists only because a departing Soyuz backed off to take it. The handshake between rival programs led directly to the ISS.
keywords: a shuttle docked to a Russian station
